chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content:
Programming Languages. (3-0). Credit 3. Exploration of the design space of
programming languages via an in-depth study of two programming languages, one
functional and one object-oriented; focuses on idiomatic uses of each language
and on features characteristic for each language. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better
in CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent
enrollment.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, students will be able to:

• Use modern programming languages effectively
• Develop the necessary skills to learn and adopt new programming languages
• Evaluate the power of programming languages and their constructs
• Apply language constructs, common abstraction mechanisms, and efficiency
considerations
• Analyze the meaning and efficiency of functional and object-oriented programs
• Master many specific language concepts and use them effectively to build correct
and elegant software

Although the course uses and studies specific programming languages, learning these
languages should be considered as a side benefit of the course, not the main goal.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
Programming in Haskell, 2nd Ed.
ISBN: 978-1316626221
Authors: Graham Hutton
Publisher: Cambridge University Press
Edition: Second Edition
URL for Resource: https://people.cs.nott.ac.uk/pszgmh/pih.html
Notes:
Reserved at the TAMU library.

The easiest way to install Haskell on your
computer: https://www.haskell.org/ghcup/

This material Is: Optional
The Java Programming Language, 4th Ed.
ISBN: 978-0-321-34980-4
Authors: Ken Arnold, James Gosling, and David Holmes
Edition: Fourth Edition
Notes:
Reserved at the TAMU library; also eBook available via the TAMU library: From the
following website > click "Sign In" > choose "Texas A&M University" from the list of
affiliated institutions > use your NetID credential to access the eBook:
https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/the-javatm-programming/0321349806/

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
One midterm exam and one comprehensive final exam, each 25%. The midterm
exam will be taken in our classroom during the class time. The final exam will be
taken in our classroom starting at the time set by the registrar. See the course
schedule below for the exam dates and times.

Homework 25%: There will be about eight sets of homework, which mainly consist
of programming, step-by-step explanation and/or analyzing the correctness and
efficiency of your program.

Quizzes 15%: There will be weekly quizzes. One lowest quiz grade will be dropped.

Exercises 10%: There will be weekly exercises. One lowest exercise grade will be
dropped.

Final grades will be assigned according to this scale based on the weighted final
average:

A: 90 and above,
B: [80–90),
C: [70–80),
D: [60–70),
F: below 60

The scale may be adjusted by the instructor to reflect score variations.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Late submission beyond the deadline will not be accepted in general, unless a
university-sanctioned excuse is provided ahead of time. Homework submission is
the only exception: late submission of homework beyond the deadline will incur 5%
deduction per hour late (Canvas rounds up to the hour). For example, if you submit
your work within one hour after the deadline, 5% of your grade will be deducted. If
you submit your work 2 hours and 2 minutes after the deadline, then your grade
will be deducted by 15%.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Midterm Exam: Thursday, 2/26 in our classroom during our class time (for 60
minutes)

Final Exam: Tuesday, 5/5, starting at 3:30 PM in our classroom (for 90 minutes)

(Tentative schedule; see Canvas Modules for more details. Reading: [H] Haskell textbook, [J]
Java textbook)

| Week # | Class Dates | Topics | Reading |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1/13, 1/15 | Introduction; Haskell and Functional Programming Basics | [H] Chs. 1, 2 |
| 2 | 1/20, 1/22 | Haskell Types, Currying, Polymorphism; Functions | [H] Chs. 3, 4, 15 |
| 3 | 127, 1/29 | List Comprehensions; Recursive Functions | [H] Chs. 5, 6, 16 |
| 4 | 2/3, 2/5 | Higher-Order Functions; Declaring Types and Classes | [H] Chs. 7, 8 |
| 5 | 2/10, 2/12 | IO; Monads; Monadic Parsing | [H] Chs. 10, 12, 13 |
| 6 | 2/17, 2/19 | Syntax and Semantics; PL Implementation | [H] Ch. 17 |
| 7 | 2/24, 2/26 | Syntactic Analysis; Midterm Review; Midterm Exam | [J] Chs. 1, 2 |
| 8 | 3/3, 3/5 | Java and Object-Oriented Programming Basics | [J] Chs. 3, 4, 5 |
| 9 | (3/9-3/13) | Spring break. No classes | |
| 10 | 3/17, 3/19 | Java and OOP Basics (Cont.) | [J] Chs. 6, 7, 8 |
| 11 | 3/24, 3/26 | Java Virtual Machine (JVM) | [J] Chs. 9, 10 |
| 12 | 3/31,4/2 | Java Generics | [J] Ch. 11 |
| 13 | 4/7, 4/9 | Java Generics (Cont.); Java Concurrency | [J] Chs. 11, 14 |

| 15 | 4/21, 4/23* | Java Reflection; Final review | [J] Ch. 16 |

(*) Our last class

chunks.title: Course Conduct and Academic Integrity
chunks.content:
Each assignment will be done
individually. Note the following acceptable/unacceptable collaborations.

Acceptable collaboration includes: (1) discussing the assigned problems to
understand their meaning or (2) discussing possible approaches to assigned
problems. However, you must explicitly acknowledge any help received from
someone and reference every source you use, whether it be a person, a book, a
paper, a solution set, a web page or whatever.

Unacceptable collaboration includes: (1) copying (verbatim use) of someone else's
solution including Al, physical papers or computer files including program files,
either wholly or in part, however you have access to them, (2) submission of
solutions that are jointly authored, or authored either wholly or in part by other
individual including Al, or (3) providing physical papers or computer files including
program files of your or third-party solutions to other individuals or posting them
on any Internet media.

In general, you may discuss the strategy and approach to solutions with others, but
you must construct and write up all final answers in your own words by
yourself, and you must not share the final product in any way even after the
deadline has passed and even after the course is over.

Make sure that all of your files are properly secured since you may be responsible if
someone copies your files.

Should questions arise during the course of working on a problem, immediately
contact the instructor by email or by office hour visit.

chunks.title: Attendance and Make-up Policy
chunks.content:
Class attendance and course participation is
mandatory. If students miss class, it is their responsibility to catch up with the
materials and announcements.

Medical reasons or other extenuating conditions beyond the control of the student
must be properly documented. Any make-up work must be completed before the
solutions are discussed or posted or the graded results become available to the
students, whichever occurs first. Discuss unusual circumstances in advance with
the instructor when possible. For more information, see https://student-
rules.tamu.edu/rule07.

chunks.title: Submission of Work Policy
chunks.content:
All assignments must be submitted electronically
using the submission link set up by the instructor on the Canvas course page by
the due date and time specified for each assignment. E-mail submissions will not
be accepted; they will be ignored without notice.

chunks.title: Regrading Policy
chunks.content:
A student can request regrading of any graded material if the
student believes that the points assigned are inconsistent with the quality and
merits of the submitted work. To request regrading the student needs to follow the
guidelines below.

• Regrading requests must be submitted to the instructor or using the regrade
request directly on Gradescope if the grading is done on Gradescope, within
one week (unless specified otherwise) after the graded results have been
released to the student. After this time limit no regrading requests will be
honored.
• Regrading requests must be in a written form using the regrade request on
Gradescope or via e-mail, accompanied by a reasonable amount of specific
justification and documentation. Make sure that you receive the confirmation
response from the instructor or the TA.

chunks.title: Course Copyright Statement
chunks.content:
All materials used in this course are copyrighted.
These materials include but are not limited to the syllabi, exercises, quizzes, exams,
homework problems, solutions provided by the instructor, lecture notes and
slides, handouts, and course videos, etc. Because these materials are copyrighted,
you do not have the right to copy or distribute these materials, unless permission
is explicitly granted by the instructor.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: Any use of Al is strictly prohibited in this course.
